author: @ScarletFlames1
text: I just realized, using a shear mixer to create heat in oil is genius because it self regulates due to the oil becoming less viscous as becomes hotter, thus less friction. You'd just need to figure out the specific oil temperatures at specific speeds and it'll be a done deal.

author: @PVflying
text: Why is a heater like this more efficient than a simple resistive element heater?

replies.author: @DrSpooglemon
replies.text: That's a good question. I think it might be that the element still has to allow electricity to flow through it and that counts as lost energy. Maybe the motor turning it is more effective at converting electricity to kinetic energy than an element is at converting electricity to heat and the motor doing the conversion to heat is converting that first to electricity which would be as efficient in the opposite direction. The electricity having no where to go is all converted to heat.

replies.author: @Beeeeeeeeeee
replies.text: Yeah this is is a very inefficient way of heating, even an incandescent lamp is probably 50x better in terms of maintenance costs than this weird contraption.

replies.author: @DrSpooglemon
replies.text: @Beeeeeeeeeee You got any numbers to back that up?

replies.author: @Beeeeeeeeeee
replies.text: @DrSpooglemon Yeah the 2nd law of thermodynamics. As long as this isn't actively pumping heat from outside to the inside, it's max efficiency is 100%, the same as a normal resistive electric heater with unlimited lifetime of operation. With his example, you have bearings that'll start wearing out and need to be replaced. That's only if he's using a induction motor to drive it, a brushed motor would wear out much faster.

author: @Buzzhumma
text: I will definitely be making a shear force device out of this motor concept . Basically all the engineering is done for me 👍🏻😂

author: @robinvince616
text: But all electrical devices are ultimately 100% efficient at converting the energy they consume into heat. Expecting anything over 100% means you're siding with the quack "Free Energy" brigade. The only use for something like this would be if your energy source was already mechanical in nature.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Have proof of concept, now will it work with a wind fan driving it, lol.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
conductive losses are 100% converted to heat and friction losses are 100% converted to heat. but only 100% to the oil if motor is oil filled and powered directly with electricity vs indirectly through another motor, but not counting that lost to the stator and disipated over its area. 

while nearly 100% efficiency is possible, its electric cost may or not be cheaper than  some fossil  fuels.

author: @cayrex
text: Try to add some graphite powder to the oil or other liquid. I see that effect when I mix my conductive ink,.... in around 2 to 3 min on high power the temperature get to 45 to 50 deg C. But when I try with the same mixer only with water (when I clean the mixer),... the water heat up to 30 deg C and also need more time. Also the density of the liquid make some effect. Try it

author: @AtlasReburdened
text: I must be misunderstanding something. I can't see how it would be any more economical than a standard electric heater. I mean, all the losses in the additional conversion of electrical to mechanical go to heat output anyway, but that would just make it as economic as an electric heater, but more expensive upfront and with bearings and a motor that have a much shorter life than a length of NiChrome wire. Is it targeted toward situations where one only has access to torque, but not electricity?

replies.author: @AndyFletcherX31
replies.text: if you have mechanical power and want heat it is a good solution. If you already have electrical power then use a resistance heater or heat pump. You could even use a heat pump if you just have mechanical power!

author: @karlmyers6518
text: I like this idea. I hope you are well

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: I like it, but you should compare it to magnetic induction heating and to resistance heating using your conductive ink.  I've thought seriously about using solar and wind heating applied to an insulated reservoir for use in a peltier/seebeck generator at need.  It seems that this experiment may be one way of turning rotational energy into friction.
